seo.title: CSS Selectors and Specificity - Complete Guide with Examples | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master CSS selectors: element, class, id, universal, attribute, descendant, child, sibling, and specificity rules. 58 practice questions with runnable examples.
seo.keywords: css selectors, css specificity, class selector, id selector, descendant selector, child selector, sibling selector, attribute selector, nth-child, css cascade

content.what:
What Are CSS Selectors?

A selector is how you tell CSS which HTML elements to style. In CSS, you write rules like h1 { color: red; } — the h1 is the selector. But there are many, many more ways to target elements than just their tag name. You can target by class, by ID, by attribute, by position in a list, by whether they are children of other elements, and much more.

Selectors are the foundation of CSS. The better you know them, the more surgical and precise your styling becomes. Instead of wrapping everything in extra divs and classes, you can use smart selectors to target exactly what you want.

What Is Specificity?

When multiple CSS rules try to style the same element, there is a conflict. Which rule wins? Specificity is the system CSS uses to pick the winner. Every selector has a specificity score. The higher score wins. An ID beats a class. A class beats an element. Inline styles beat almost everything.
content.why:
Why Should You Learn Selectors and Specificity?

1. Precise Targeting

Simple selectors like p style every paragraph on the page. But what if you only want to style paragraphs inside a specific section? Or the first paragraph after a heading? Or every other row in a table? With advanced selectors, you can do all of that without adding extra classes.

2. Clean HTML

If you rely only on classes, your HTML ends up full of them: class="card card-large card-blue card-featured". Good selectors let you keep HTML clean and put the complexity in CSS where it belongs.

3. Understanding Specificity Saves Hours

Every CSS developer eventually hits a moment where their rule does not apply, and they have no idea why. Nine times out of ten, it is specificity. Understanding the rules lets you debug it in seconds instead of hours.

4. Reading Other People's CSS

Every CSS codebase uses selectors. If you want to read Bootstrap, Tailwind output, or any real-world CSS, you need to know what the selectors mean.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Element Selector

Targets all elements of a certain tag name. Simplest selector.

p { color: blue; }        /* all paragraphs */
h1 { font-size: 32px; }   /* all h1 elements */
button { cursor: pointer; } /* all buttons */

2. Class Selector

Targets elements with a specific class attribute. Start with a period ..

.highlight { background: yellow; }
.big { font-size: 24px; }

In HTML: <p class="highlight">This is highlighted</p>. Classes can be reused on many elements.

3. ID Selector

Targets the one element with a specific id. Start with a hash #.

#header { background: black; }
#main { padding: 20px; }

IDs should be unique on a page — only one element should have the same id.

4. Universal Selector

The asterisk * targets every element on the page. Useful for resetting default browser styles.

* {
  margin: 0;
  padding: 0;
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

5. Attribute Selector

Targets elements with a specific attribute. Use square brackets.

[type="text"] { border: 1px solid grey; }
[disabled] { opacity: 0.5; }
a[target="_blank"] { color: red; }

This lets you style elements based on their attributes without needing classes.

6. Descendant Selector (Space)

A descendant selector uses a space between two selectors. It matches elements that are inside other elements, at any depth.

ul li { color: blue; }  /* every li inside a ul, at any depth */
article p { font-size: 18px; }  /* every p inside an article */

7. Child Selector (>)

The child combinator > is like the descendant selector, but only matches direct children — not grandchildren.

ul > li { list-style: square; }
/* Only direct li children of ul, not nested li inside inner lists */

8. Adjacent Sibling Selector (+)

The plus sign + matches an element that comes immediately after another element, and both share the same parent.

h1 + p { font-weight: bold; }
/* The first paragraph right after every h1 becomes bold */

9. General Sibling Selector (~)

The tilde ~ matches all siblings that come after an element, not just the immediate one.

h1 ~ p { color: grey; }
/* Every p that comes after an h1 (sharing the same parent) becomes grey */

10. Grouping (Comma)

Separate multiple selectors with commas to apply the same rule to all of them.

h1, h2, h3 { font-family: Georgia; }
.button, .link, input[type="submit"] { cursor: pointer; }

Specificity Rules

Specificity is calculated as a score with four parts (inline, ID, class, element). Here are the values:

  • Inline style (style="...") — specificity 1000
  • ID selector (#main) — specificity 100
  • Class, attribute, pseudo-class (.btn, [type], :hover) — specificity 10
  • Element, pseudo-element (p, ::before) — specificity 1

When selectors combine, add up the specificity:

  • p = 1
  • .note = 10
  • #main = 100
  • #main p = 101
  • #main .note = 110
  • .card p.bold = 21 (class + element + class)

The !important Flag (and Why to Avoid It)

If you add !important to a CSS value, it overrides normal specificity rules:

p { color: red !important; }

But do not use !important unless you really have to. It makes CSS hard to maintain because now you cannot override it without another !important. Every new !important piles on the mess. Better specificity is almost always a cleaner solution.

The Cascade and Inheritance

The cascade (which gives CSS its name) decides which rule wins. The factors, in order:

  1. Importance (!important values win)
  2. Specificity (higher wins)
  3. Source order (later wins when specificity is equal)

Inheritance is when a child element takes on styles from its parent. Some properties (like color and font-family) are inherited by default. Others (like margin and border) are not. This is why setting font-family on body automatically applies to everything inside.

codeExamples.title: Element, Class, and ID Selectors
codeExamples.code:
Aarav's Page

This is a normal paragraph.

This paragraph has the highlight class.

Another normal paragraph.

Another highlighted one!
codeExamples.explanation: Three selectors in action. The p element selector colors all paragraphs dark grey. The .highlight class selector gives yellow backgrounds to any element with that class. The #main-title ID selector styles just the one element with that ID. Save as selectors.html and open.
codeExamples.output: A centered purple heading, two normal grey paragraphs, and two yellow-highlighted paragraphs.

codeExamples.title: Descendant and Child Selectors
codeExamples.code:
Descendant Selector (every li inside ul)

  • Item 1
    • Nested item 1 (also blue — deep descendant)
  • Item 2

Child Selector (only direct children)

  • Direct child (bold red)
    • Nested (NOT bold red — it is a grandchild)
  • Direct child (bold red)
codeExamples.explanation: This shows the difference between descendant (space) and child (>) selectors. ul li matches every li inside a ul, even nested ones. .direct-only > li matches only li that are DIRECT children of the ul — not grandchildren (like li inside a nested ul). Both lists look different because of this.
codeExamples.output: First list has all items blue (including nested). Second list has only the top-level items bold red; the nested item is just blue.

codeExamples.title: Adjacent and General Sibling Selectors
codeExamples.code:
Main Heading

This is the first paragraph after h1 (bold green).

This one is NOT — it is the second paragraph.

Second Heading

First paragraph (italic red — it is a sibling of h2).

Second paragraph (also italic red — also a sibling).

Third paragraph (still red — general sibling selector).
codeExamples.explanation: h1 + p only matches the FIRST p immediately after an h1. h2 ~ p matches EVERY p that comes after an h2 and shares the same parent. These are powerful for styling specific patterns without needing extra classes.
codeExamples.output: First paragraph after h1 is bold green. Next paragraph is normal. After h2, all three paragraphs are italic red.

codeExamples.title: Attribute Selectors
codeExamples.code:
Secure link (green)

Download PDF (red bold)
codeExamples.explanation: Attribute selectors use square brackets. [type="text"] matches elements with that exact attribute value. [required] matches elements that simply have the attribute. [href^="https"] matches elements whose href STARTS with 'https'. [href$=".pdf"] matches elements whose href ENDS with '.pdf'. Incredibly useful for styling based on attributes.
codeExamples.output: Three input fields with different border colors for text (blue), email (green), and password (red), all with yellow backgrounds because of [required]. Two links: Google in green, the PDF in bold red.

codeExamples.title: Grouping Selectors with Commas
codeExamples.code:
Biggest

Big

Medium
codeExamples.explanation: Commas group selectors so they all share the same rules. This is cleaner than writing the same rule six times for h1 through h6. You can even mix different selector types in one group: a class, an ID, and an attribute selector all get the same button styles.
codeExamples.output: Three differently-sized headings all in dark blue Georgia font. Three buttons (from class, id, and input) all styled identically in blue.

codeExamples.title: Specificity in Action
codeExamples.code:
Plain paragraph (black)

Has .blue-text class (blue)

Has .green-text class (green)

Has ID #special AND class .blue-text — ID wins (purple, yellow bg)

ID + extra class — red wins
codeExamples.explanation: This demonstrates specificity in action. Even though the second-to-last paragraph has the class .blue-text, the ID selector #special wins because ID specificity (100) is higher than class specificity (10). The last paragraph has an even more specific selector that wins out.
codeExamples.output: Five paragraphs showing how higher specificity wins: plain black, blue (class), green (class+element), purple with yellow bg (ID beats class), red (ID+class beats everything).

codeExamples.title: Targeting Every Other li with nth-child
codeExamples.code:
Student Attendance (Class 8A)

  • Aarav Sharma
  • Priya Patel
  • Rohan Kumar
  • Ananya Singh
  • Vikram Gupta
  • Meera Reddy
codeExamples.explanation: Pseudo-class selectors like :nth-child(even) let you target elements based on their position. :nth-child(even) picks every 2nd, 4th, 6th... element. :first-child picks the first, :last-child picks the last. You can also use :nth-child(3) for a specific position, or :nth-child(3n) for every third.
codeExamples.output: A list of students where alternating rows have different background colors (zebra stripes). The first student is bold dark blue; the last is italic red.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting the Dot for Class Selectors
commonMistakes.error: This targets HTML tags called <highlight>, which do not exist. No styles apply.
commonMistakes.explanation: Class selectors must start with a period (.). Without it, CSS thinks you are targeting an element tag with that name. Always write .classname for classes.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting the Hash for ID Selectors
commonMistakes.error: This tries to target an element tag <main-title> which doesn't exist.
commonMistakes.explanation: ID selectors must start with a hash (#). Without it, you are targeting a non-existent element.

commonMistakes.title: Using the Same ID Twice
commonMistakes.error: No HTML error, but this is invalid. IDs should be unique per page.
commonMistakes.explanation: IDs should be unique on a page. If you need to style multiple elements the same way, use a class instead. Classes can be reused as many times as you want; IDs cannot.

commonMistakes.title: Overusing !important
commonMistakes.error: No error, but now both rules are 'important' and it gets confusing. If you want to override, you need more !important, leading to a mess.
commonMistakes.explanation: !important should be a last resort. Overusing it makes CSS impossible to maintain. Instead, use more specific selectors (like adding a class or an ID to the selector) to override earlier rules naturally.

commonMistakes.title: Using Descendant When You Meant Child
commonMistakes.error: Unintentionally styles li inside nested sub-menus too.
commonMistakes.explanation: A space (descendant) matches elements at any depth. > (child) matches only direct children. For dropdown menus and nested lists, the difference is huge — descendant will accidentally style nested items too.

summary: CSS selectors let you target HTML elements by tag, class, id, attribute, position, and relationship to other elements.
Element selectors (p, h1, div) target all elements of that tag. They have specificity 1.
Class selectors (.highlight, .button) target elements with that class attribute. Classes can be reused. Specificity 10.
ID selectors (#main, #header) target one unique element. IDs must be unique per page. Specificity 100.
The universal selector * matches every element. Useful for CSS resets.
Attribute selectors ([type="text"], [required], [href^="https"]) let you style based on attributes without extra classes.
Descendant selector (space): ul li means every li inside a ul, at any depth. Child selector (>): ul > li means only direct children.
Adjacent sibling (+): h1 + p means the first p right after an h1. General sibling (~): h2 ~ p means every p after an h2.
Group selectors with commas: h1, h2, h3 applies the same rule to all three.
Specificity: inline (1000) > ID (100) > class/attr/pseudo-class (10) > element/pseudo-element (1). Higher wins.
The !important flag overrides normal specificity. Avoid using it — it makes CSS hard to maintain.
The cascade resolves conflicts using: importance, then specificity, then source order (later wins when equal). Inheritance passes some properties (color, font) from parent to child.

topicSpecific.question: Which selector targets all paragraphs?
topicSpecific.explanation: The element selector p targets all <p> elements.

topicSpecific.question: Which symbol starts a class selector?
topicSpecific.explanation: Class selectors start with a dot: .highlight { ... }

topicSpecific.question: Which symbol starts an ID selector?
topicSpecific.explanation: ID selectors start with a hash: #main { ... }

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS to make all elements with class 'danger' red.
topicSpecific.explanation: Class selectors target elements with class="danger".

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS to style the element with id 'header' with a blue background.
topicSpecific.explanation: ID selectors start with # and target the unique element with that id.

topicSpecific.question: Which has higher specificity: p or .note?
topicSpecific.explanation: Classes have specificity 10, elements have specificity 1. The class wins.

topicSpecific.question: Which selector matches every element on the page?
topicSpecific.explanation: The universal selector * matches every element. Often used in CSS resets.

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS to remove the margin and padding from every element on the page.
topicSpecific.explanation: The universal selector resets margin and padding on every element. This is a classic CSS reset.

topicSpecific.question: What does ul li mean?
topicSpecific.answer: Every <li> that is inside a <ul>, at any depth.
topicSpecific.explanation: A space between selectors is the descendant selector. It matches elements inside other elements, even if deeply nested.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between ul li and ul > li?
topicSpecific.answer: ul li matches all li inside ul at any depth. ul > li matches only DIRECT li children of ul.
topicSpecific.explanation: The space is descendant (any depth). The > is child (direct children only). This difference matters when there are nested lists.

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS to style all paragraphs inside articles with a grey color.
topicSpecific.explanation: article p targets every p inside an article tag.

topicSpecific.question: What is the specificity of #main?
topicSpecific.explanation: ID selectors always have a specificity of 100.

topicSpecific.answer: Missing dot. Should be .highlight { color: yellow; }
topicSpecific.explanation: Without the dot, CSS thinks you are targeting a tag called highlight, which does not exist.

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS to group h1, h2, and h3 and give them all a red color.
topicSpecific.explanation: Commas group selectors. The same rule applies to all three.

topicSpecific.question: What does h1 + p match?
topicSpecific.answer: The first <p> element that comes immediately after an <h1>, sharing the same parent.
topicSpecific.explanation: The + combinator targets the next immediate sibling. Only the very first p after each h1 is matched.

topicSpecific.question: What does h1 ~ p match?
topicSpecific.answer: Every <p> that comes after an <h1>, sharing the same parent (not just the first one).
topicSpecific.explanation: The ~ combinator targets all following siblings. Every p after the h1 gets styled, not just the immediately next one.

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS to target only the li elements that are direct children of nav (not nested ones).
topicSpecific.explanation: The > ensures only direct children are styled, not nested li elements inside sub-menus.

topicSpecific.question: Which of these has higher specificity: #main or .btn.large?
topicSpecific.explanation: One ID beats two classes. IDs are 100, each class is 10, so .btn.large is only 20.

topicSpecific.question: Write a complete HTML page where every other list item has a grey background using nth-child.
topicSpecific.explanation: :nth-child(even) targets every 2nd, 4th, 6th item. The result is a zebra stripe pattern.

topicSpecific.question: Given .red { color: red; } and p { color: blue; }, what color is <p class="red">Text</p>?
topicSpecific.answer: Red. The class (10) beats the element (1).
topicSpecific.explanation: Class specificity 10 beats element specificity 1. The paragraph will be red.

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS using attribute selector to make all email inputs have a green border.
topicSpecific.explanation: Attribute selectors let you style based on attribute values without needing classes.

topicSpecific.hint: Actually, it might work — analyse it.
topicSpecific.answer: The CSS is valid. It targets every li that is a descendant of a direct li child of ul. So it styles nested li elements.
topicSpecific.explanation: This is a complex selector: direct li children of ul, then any li descendant of those. Valid but uncommon. There may be no bug if the HTML is structured to match.

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS targeting the first li in a list using the :first-child pseudo-class.
topicSpecific.explanation: :first-child targets the first element of a specific type within its parent.

topicSpecific.question: What is the specificity of #main .card p?
topicSpecific.explanation: ID (100) + class (10) + element (1) = 111.

topicSpecific.question: Build a complete HTML page where links to PDFs show in bold red with a 'PDF' label automatically. Use an attribute selector.
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
  a[href$=".pdf"] {
    color: red;
    font-weight: bold;
  }
</style>
</head>
<body>
  <p><a href="https://example.com">Regular link</a></p>
  <p><a href="document.pdf">Download PDF</a></p>
</body>
</html>
topicSpecific.explanation: [href$=".pdf"] matches href values that END with '.pdf'. Great for styling download links automatically.

topicSpecific.question: Given: p { color: red; } p.note { color: blue; } #special { color: green; }. What color is <p id="special" class="note">Text</p>?
topicSpecific.answer: Green. #special has specificity 100, p.note has 11, p has 1. #special wins.
topicSpecific.explanation: ID (100) > class+element (11) > element (1). The ID rule wins.

topicSpecific.question: Write CSS to target every third item in a list using nth-child.
topicSpecific.explanation: :nth-child(3n) targets the 3rd, 6th, 9th items (every multiple of 3).

topicSpecific.answer: Inline styles have specificity 1000, which beats the class's 10. Inline CSS wins.
topicSpecific.explanation: Inline styles override class and ID selectors unless you use !important (not recommended).

mixed.question: What does the universal selector * target?
mixed.explanation: The asterisk matches all elements. Often used for CSS resets.

mixed.question: Write a class selector to make all elements with class 'big' have a 24px font size.
mixed.explanation: The .big class selector targets all elements with class="big".

mixed.question: Can multiple elements share the same class?
mixed.answer: Yes, classes are designed to be reused on many elements.
mixed.explanation: Unlike IDs, classes are meant to be applied to multiple elements. That is their main advantage.

mixed.question: Can multiple elements share the same ID?
mixed.answer: No, IDs must be unique on a page.
mixed.explanation: IDs are meant to be unique. If you need to style multiple elements the same way, use a class.

mixed.question:
Why is this class selector wrong?
#highlight { color: yellow; }
mixed.answer: If highlight is a class, use .highlight with a dot, not #.
mixed.explanation: # is for IDs, . is for classes. Always match the symbol to the attribute type.

mixed.question: Write CSS to style all elements with class 'card' AND also have the class 'featured'.
mixed.hint: Chain the class selectors without a space.
mixed.explanation: Chaining selectors (no space) means the element must match BOTH. Specificity is 20 (two classes).

mixed.question: What is the specificity of .btn.large?
mixed.explanation: Each class is 10. Two classes chained = 20.

mixed.question: Write a CSS rule to target all buttons that have the 'disabled' attribute.
mixed.explanation: Attribute selectors target elements with specific attributes. [disabled] matches elements that simply have the attribute.

mixed.question: What does a[href^="https"] match?
mixed.answer: All <a> tags whose href attribute starts with 'https'.
mixed.explanation: ^= means starts with. This is useful for styling secure links differently.

mixed.question:
The second-level list items are also being styled, but Aarav only wants the top-level ones. Fix:
.menu li { color: red; }
mixed.answer: Use .menu > li to target only direct children.
mixed.explanation: The descendant selector (space) matches at any depth. The child combinator (>) matches only direct children.

mixed.question: Write a complete HTML page styling the first letter of a paragraph in red with a bigger font. Use ::first-letter pseudo-element.
mixed.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
  p::first-letter {
    color: red;
    font-size: 32px;
    font-weight: bold;
  }
</style>
</head>
<body>
  <p>This paragraph has a fancy first letter.</p>
</body>
</html>
mixed.explanation: Pseudo-elements like ::first-letter style parts of an element. Useful for drop-cap effects.

mixed.question: Given: p { color: red !important; } #id p { color: blue; }. What wins?
mixed.explanation: !important beats higher specificity. Even though #id p is more specific, the !important red wins. This is why !important is dangerous.

mixed.question: Build a complete HTML page with a styled nav menu where only the FIRST link is blue (use :first-child).
mixed.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
  nav ul { list-style: none; display: flex; gap: 20px; }
  nav li:first-child a { color: blue; font-weight: bold; }
</style>
</head>
<body>
  <nav>
    <ul>
      <li><a href="/">Home</a></li>
      <li><a href="/about">About</a></li>
      <li><a href="/contact">Contact</a></li>
    </ul>
  </nav>
</body>
</html>
mixed.explanation: li:first-child a targets the anchor inside the first li. Only the 'Home' link is blue and bold.

mixed.question: What is the specificity of body #main .card p.bold?
mixed.hint: Count each type: 1 element + 1 ID + 2 classes + 1 element.
mixed.explanation: Element (1) + ID (100) + class (10) + class (10) + element (1) = 122. Correction: 1+100+10+10+1 = 122.

mixed.answer: #main p has specificity 101, .override has 10. The ID wins. To fix, use #main p.override or add more specificity.
mixed.explanation: To override a selector with an ID, you need equal or higher specificity. Use #main p.override { color: blue; } which has specificity 111.

mcqs.question: Which symbol starts a class selector?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Class selectors start with a dot: .classname. The # is for IDs.

mcqs.question: Which symbol starts an ID selector?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. ID selectors start with a hash: #idname. The . is for classes.

mcqs.question: What does the universal selector * do?

options.B: Targets all elements on the page

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The asterisk matches every element. Used in CSS resets to clear default margins and padding.

mcqs.question: Which has higher specificity: a class or an element selector?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Classes have specificity 10, elements have specificity 1. Classes win when both apply.

mcqs.question: What does ul li target?

options.A: Only the first li in ul
options.B: All li inside ul at any depth
options.C: Only direct li children of ul

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The space is the descendant selector, matching elements at any depth inside their ancestor.

mcqs.question: What does ul > li target?

options.B: Only direct li children of ul

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The > combinator means 'direct child only'. Nested li elements are not matched.

mcqs.question: How do you group selectors to share the same rule?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Commas group selectors: h1, h2, h3 { color: red; }

mcqs.question: Can multiple HTML elements share the same ID?

options.B: No - IDs must be unique per page

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. IDs are meant to be unique. If you need to target multiple elements, use a class instead.

mcqs.question: What is the specificity of an element selector like p?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Element selectors have specificity 1. Classes are 10, IDs are 100, inline is 1000.

mcqs.question: What is the specificity of a class selector like .btn?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Classes, attributes, and pseudo-classes all have specificity 10.

mcqs.question: What is the specificity of an ID selector like #main?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. ID selectors have specificity 100. Only inline styles and !important can normally override them.

mcqs.question: Which selector targets all input elements with type='email'?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Attribute selectors use square brackets: input[type='email']

mcqs.question: What does h1 + p select?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. The + is the adjacent sibling combinator. It matches the FIRST p immediately after each h1.

mcqs.question: What does h1 ~ p select?

options.B: All paragraphs after h1 (sharing the same parent)

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The ~ is the general sibling combinator. It matches every p that comes after an h1 (same parent).

mcqs.question: Which has higher specificity: .btn or button?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Class (10) beats element (1). .btn wins.

mcqs.question: What is the specificity of #main .card p?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. 1 ID (100) + 1 class (10) + 1 element (1) = 111.

mcqs.question: What does li:nth-child(even) target?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. :nth-child(even) matches even-numbered children (2nd, 4th, 6th, etc.).

mcqs.question:
Which of the following rules applies to <p class="red" id="special">Hi</p>:
.red { color: red; }
#special { color: blue; }
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. #special has specificity 100, .red has 10. The ID wins. Blue paragraph.

mcqs.question: What does a[href^="https"] target?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. ^= means 'starts with'. Use $= for 'ends with' and *= for 'contains'.

mcqs.question: What is the specificity of an inline style?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Inline styles have specificity 1000. Only !important can override them.

mcqs.question: Which statement about !important is TRUE?

options.A: It has no effect on specificity
options.B: It overrides normal specificity and should be used often
options.C: It overrides normal specificity but should be avoided
options.D: It only works in Internet Explorer

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. !important overrides normal specificity, but overusing it leads to unmaintainable CSS. Use proper specificity instead.

mcqs.question: When two CSS rules have the same specificity, which wins?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. With equal specificity, the later rule wins. This is why order matters in CSS files.

mcqs.question: What is the specificity of .nav > li.active a?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. 1 class (10) + 1 element (1) + 1 class (10) + 1 element (1) = 22. Remember that > is not counted.

mcqs.question: Which property is NOT inherited by default?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Margin, padding, border, background — these are not inherited. Text-related properties (color, font, line-height) are inherited.

mcqs.question: Which of the following has the HIGHEST specificity?
mcqs.explanation: D is correct. Inline styles have specificity 1000, beating all the others. A has 111, B has 40, C has 105.

codingChallenges.title: Class and ID Styling
codingChallenges.description: Build a page with 3 paragraphs. One uses id 'intro' (blue), one uses class 'warning' (red), and one has no class or id (default black).
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Three paragraphs in blue, red, and black.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use both class and ID selectors. Internal CSS.
codingChallenges.solution:
Welcome to the page!

Warning: Please save your work.

This is a normal paragraph.

codingChallenges.title: Descendant Selector

codingChallenges.title: Attribute Selector for Inputs
codingChallenges.description: Build a form with text, email, password, and number inputs. Use attribute selectors to style each type with a different border color (blue, green, red, purple).
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Four inputs with differently-colored borders based on type.
codingChallenges.solution:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>Attribute Selectors</title>
  <style>
    body { font-family: Arial; padding: 30px; }
    label { display: block; margin-top: 10px; }
    input { padding: 8px; font-size: 16px; }
    input[type="text"] { border: 2px solid blue; }
    input[type="email"] { border: 2px solid green; }
    input[type="password"] { border: 2px solid red; }
    input[type="number"] { border: 2px solid purple; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <form>
    <label>Name: <input type="text"></label>
    <label>Email: <input type="email"></label>
    <label>Password: <input type="password"></label>
    <label>Age: <input type="number"></label>
  </form>
</body>
</html>

codingChallenges.title: Specificity Demo
codingChallenges.description: Build a page that demonstrates specificity: create one paragraph that has an ID, a class, and an inline style. Each rule specifies a different color. Show which one wins.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A paragraph where the inline style wins over the ID and class.
codingChallenges.constraints: Include 4 different color rules at different specificities. Comment each rule with its specificity score.
codingChallenges.solution:
Plain (black, specificity 1)

Class only (green, specificity 10)

ID only (purple, specificity 100)

All + inline (red wins, specificity 1000)
